content:
20 min read
Just now

--

Add a method here because “it’s user-related” — repeat 40 times = god object. The pressures that create it + the fix that works.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Eric Prouzet on UnsplashNobody sets out to build a 2,000-line class. It just happens.
Here’s the typical origin story. A team starts a new PHP project. Someone creates a UserService — a clean class with a handful of methods: register, login, updateProfile, deactivate. Maybe 200 lines. Reads well. Tests cover it. Good code.
A month later, a new feature ships: password reset. It uses users. Where should the logic go? UserService has methods for users. The new methods fit there. Now it's 350 lines.
A quarter later, email verification. Notifications. Two-factor authentication. Session management. Each addition feels reasonable — nobody wants to create a whole new class for three methods that “obviously” belong with the user stuff.
Two years in, UserService is 2,000 lines. The constructor has 17 dependencies. Every senior engineer knows the class needs to be broken up. Nobody has the time to do it. New features still land there because that's where user-related code lives. Testing is painful — every test needs to mock a dozen dependencies. Bugs happen because changes in one method break unrelated behavior. The team's velocity on user-related features has…
